verses.commentary: Boasting is necessary though unprofitable. Paul moves to visions and revelations, the ultimate credential in ancient Mediterranean religious competition.

A man in Christ caught up to the third heaven fourteen years ago, whether in body or out Paul doesn't know. The third-person narration creates distance; uncertainty about embodiment emphasizes the experience's transcendence.
Repetition of whether in body or out, God knows. The double emphasis on not knowing underscores that the experience exceeded normal categories of consciousness.
Caught up to paradise, heard inexpressible things no one is permitted to tell. Paradise and third heaven are equated. The content remains sealed; boasting about it is impossible.
Paul will boast about that man but not about himself except in weaknesses. The grammatical distinction between 'that man' and 'myself' creates ironic distance from his own experience.
If he chose to boast, he wouldn't be foolish because he'd speak truth. But he refrains so no one will think more of him than what they see and hear. Self-limitation protects against overestimation.
To keep from becoming conceited because of surpassingly great revelations, a thorn in the flesh was given, a messenger of Satan to torment. God uses satanic affliction to prevent pride.
Three times Paul pleaded with the Lord to take it away. The number echoes Jesus in Gethsemane; the prayer remains unanswered in the requested way.
My grace is sufficient for you, for power is perfected in weakness. God's response refuses removal but promises sustaining grace. Weakness becomes the optimal condition for divine power's display.
Delighting in weaknesses, insults, hardships, persecutions, difficulties for Christ's sake. When weak, then strong. The paradox is embraced: weakness is the pathway to strength.
The Corinthians drove Paul to foolish boasting. They should have commended him. Despite being nothing, he is not inferior to the super-apostles. Nothing and not inferior coexist.
Signs, wonders, and miracles were performed among them with perseverance. The marks of true apostleship include supernatural validation, though Paul mentions this reluctantly.
How were they inferior to other churches except Paul was never a burden? Ironic request for forgiveness for the 'wrong' of not taking their money.
Ready for the third visit, Paul will not be a burden. He wants them, not their possessions. Parental metaphor: parents save for children, not vice versa.
Paul will gladly spend everything and expend himself for them. If he loves more, will he be loved less? The question reveals relational asymmetry and vulnerability.
Granted, he wasn't a burden. Yet crafty fellow that he is, he caught them by trickery. Paul voices the accusation that his refusal of money was manipulative strategy.
Did Paul exploit them through any messengers? The rhetorical question defends against charges that he used intermediaries for financial gain.
Titus didn't exploit them, did he? Titus and the brother walked in the same Spirit by the same steps. Consistency across representatives proves integrity.
Have they been thinking Paul has been defending himself? Everything is spoken in God's sight as those in Christ, and it's all for their strengthening. Defense is actually pastoral care.

Paul fears finding them not as he wishes and being found by them not as they wish. The catalog of sins (discord, jealousy, rage, ambition, slander, gossip, arrogance, disorder) reveals dysfunction he may encounter.
Fear that God will humble him before them and he'll grieve over many who sinned and haven't repented of impurity, sexual sin, and debauchery. Unrepented sin produces apostolic grief and humiliation.
